quotes.quote: Why can't I take a full swing on the driver without slicing? If I take a lot slower swing it goes straight(about 250 yards) but when I swing faster it slices. The harder I swing the more it slices. I feel i'm leaving some distance by slowing the swing and would like to be free to take a full back swing and accellerate as fast as possible through the swing, what do I need to do?
if you're that person that hits a slice on one tea and then it's a hook on the next tea I mean we've all been there you know't we it's very depressing

a lot of golfers don't realize that quite often a hook and a slice are caused by exactly the same Bol 100% And it's really sad because then people go oh I fixed my slice but they at the root cause of it is still there they've just found the compensation so they're playing army golf right left oh fixed it right left middle

i used to have the same prob if i swung hard i would slice i think it was cuz if i gripped to hard it would make my wrist turn the club face different but now i just do a nice slow and steady drive and get it straight usually
i found that the easier i swung the better my shot looked on all shots not just the driver the harder everything would go whereever
join the club! I agree with the poster above, swinging harder usually means using your arms more at the top of your backswing. I do this when i try to hit my driver harder.
'Twas Harvey Penick who said the slicer's at the top of his game, the harder he swings the more the ball will slice.

swinging hard usually means swing top-down instead of bottom up and trying to generate extra power through your shoulders and hands instead of through your legs and hips. Almost all great golf swing starts form the bottom up with hips rotation seeting the speed and the rest of the body following. my guess is that in your hard and easy swings, your hips are moving at close to the same speed. As a result, when the upper body moves faster the club has nowhere to go but an outside in swing path
I think that when you try to hit i harder there is a good chance you are gripping the club tighter which wont let your hands come through.

This is the siren call we all should ignore but rarely do: more distance off the tee; booming 280yd+ drives. I'm more guilty at this than most, and the irony is that when I swing 'within myself' I'm farther down the fairway and in better position.

But it's deceptive. If you're 240 and in the fairway where would you be if you really swung the club as hard as you can (or slightly harder than you did)? Or what's worse is when your playing partners are driving the ball 20, 30, 40, or more yards past you in the fairway. After a few holes like that you start trying to keep up and without even thinking about it you start swinging harder, and we all know what happens when you do that.
I've had to accept that fact that I'm not a long hitter of the ball. But even having done that it'll all fall apart on me whenever I try to really belt one out there.

And something that makes this even harder to take is the occassional time when I get away with it and connect on one of those drives I'm trying to hit. That's the worst case of all because then I'll start chasing that ellusive distance all over again to the overall detriment of my game.
Your arms and body get out of synch when you swing harder.

we're all men... once we get on the first tee box... everything we practiced on the range goes out the window.... we try and become the long driving champ... i know thats how i am sometimes... GRIP IT AND RIP IT!!!
I had this problem I slowed down my swing and am hitting straight 280 yards and hitting your 2nd shot from the other fairway. im still getting 240 off the tee and hitting it from my own fairway
if you can hit it straight 250 why would you want to swing faster anyway?

I agree. That's what my instructor tells me. Ever since I've loosened my grip I actually get a nice draw. Mind you I do lose distance. He keeps telling me its better to be 250 in the fairway than 300 in the woods or worst.

it SOUNDS to me like you're coming over the top - your first move down is 'casting' with your arms. Coming over the top opens the club face (slice-er-roni). The faster you swing the more the face opens
as you swing harder, your arms are getting so far ahead of the clubhead that the face just isn't squaring up at impact. The faster you swing, the more the problem is exaserbated.
you do want to try to correct that slice because it takes a lot of distance off it does take a lot of distance a lot of distance
you can play with a slice it is it is hard though cuz sometimes you get that hook instead
nothing worse than not knowing which way it's going to go next
it's out of bounds on the right on that hole and you're like well I'm not over there

which is really hard to do when the ball's been going to the right and and your coach says swing more to the right you go you're an idiot I'm not swinging to the right the ball's already going to the right that's why it's so hard to fix the slide

people that slice it go back um more to the inside roll the club in which isn't you know ideal but then they're a little bit behind themselves and then their body knows they got to get forward so they're kind of coming over the top and they have this like pulling over the top sensation and coming into the ball
you go and try this on the golf course you'll be taking our names in vain in a very short space of time
You aim dead center and the ball sails into the trees. Does this sound familiar?
If you've ever hit a hook or a slice and had no idea why it happened, this will finally make sense.

My experience with students is that when somebody comes in with a face that's 14 degrees open and I have them close the club down 45 degrees and have them hit the golf ball, a lot of times we don't see an extreme hook. We see a ball that goes very very straight

number one I want to establish why you're slicing it you're slicing it because you're coming into the ball from the outside and over the top so we're going to take it back tendency wise people that slice it go back um more to the inside roll the club in which isn't you know ideal
struggling with a slice or dealing with unpredictable hooks, this video provides crucial insights to straighten out your shots
Stop playing 'army golf' and start hitting more fairways!
A good player hooks it 15 yards into a fairaway bunker, a higher handicap slices it into the trees, a beginner tops or whiffs completely.
However, a miss hit will be amplified more than a slower swing.
On the other hand if you swing slower than what feels natural to you, you may be off your personal rhythm. At that point you might manipulate your swing, rather than letting the club swing freely.

I have a very high swing speed, and while it Looks like I'm swinging out of my shoes sometimes to some people, it is in rhythm and balance. However, a miss hit will be amplified more than a slower swing.

You will know you're in rhythm when most of the force of the swing is just before impact. A great way to test this is to turn your club upside-down (gripping under the clubhead, swinging the grip). Listen for a 'wooosh' sound. You want that just before impact, maybe 12-18 inches.
Swing too fast on the way back and they all fly out all over the park. Noone wants that,lol. Don't leave all your power behind your head, at parallel..
But you can ruin an otherwise beautiful swing with poor rhythm.
The slice is one of the most frustrating faults in golf - and it almost always comes from an over-the-top move.
The slice usually starts with an over-the-top move - opening your shoulders and hips too early in the downswing.
and we don't have control of the club face because it's moving so quickly in the swing we think we do but we don't
you're still swinging out to in but now you close the club face so you're swinging across with a closed Club face then it's almost a smothering shot to the left
when you hold it open your slice and when you get sick of seeing it slice you close it down

then you find the exact right amount that you have your Club face Square we talked about square square to the way you're swinging left and it might go straight but that's a pretty damn hard thing to do
if i swung hard i would slice i think it was cuz if i gripped to hard it would make my wrist turn the club face different
I don't think my swing angle or mechanics changer when I swing hard,it just slices more.

the scorecard does not care how far you drive it, granted a longer drive can help you score better. However, 250 in the fairway regularly will give you more scoring ops than 260 in the right rough (or forest, lake, etc.).
as a 20+ hcp who can hit his drive 250 and straight, you should spend your time getting the rest of your game to that same high level of performance
